questions.question: Explain why shadows are not formed when light is blocked partially.
questions.answer: Shadows are formed where light is completely blocked. If the object allows some light to pass, the shadow becomes faint or partial.

questions.question: Describe how translucent objects affect shadows.
questions.answer: Translucent objects allow some light to pass through, so the shadows formed are lighter and less distinct than those of opaque objects.

questions.question: Explain why shadow length changes during different times of the day.
questions.answer: As the Sun moves, the angle of incoming light changes. Shadows are longer when the Sun is low in the sky and shorter when it is overhead.

questions.question: Describe the image formed by a plane mirror.
questions.answer: A plane mirror forms a virtual, upright image that is the same size as the object but laterally inverted.

questions.question: Explain why diffused reflection is important for seeing objects.
questions.answer: Diffused reflection scatters light in many directions, allowing us to see objects from different angles rather than just in one direction.

questions.question: How do concave mirrors form magnified images for near objects?
questions.answer: Concave mirrors converge light rays from nearby objects, forming a virtual, magnified image in front of the mirror.

questions.question: Explain why convex mirrors are used for rear-view mirrors in vehicles.
questions.answer: Convex mirrors diverge light rays, giving a wider field of view and allowing drivers to see more area behind the vehicle.

questions.question: Describe the principle behind a periscope.
questions.answer: A periscope uses two plane mirrors at 45° angles to reflect light from a higher position down to the viewer's eyes, allowing observation without exposure.

questions.question: Explain why multiple shadows may appear with multiple light sources.
questions.answer: Each light source casts its own shadow. When more than one light is present, shadows overlap, creating multiple shadows.

questions.question: How does a pinhole camera produce an image?
questions.answer: Light rays pass through a small pinhole and project an inverted image of the object on the screen inside the camera.

questions.question: Explain why the shadow of an object is darkest at noon.
questions.answer: At noon, the Sun is directly overhead, so light is most concentrated and shadows receive the least scattered light, making them darkest.

questions.question: Describe the effect of moving an object closer to a light source on its shadow.
questions.answer: Moving an object closer to the light makes the shadow larger and more defined because the light rays diverge less before hitting the object.

questions.question: Why are opaque objects used to make sundials?
questions.answer: Opaque objects block sunlight completely, creating clear shadows that indicate the position of the Sun for telling time.

questions.question: Explain why images in a plane mirror appear behind the mirror.
questions.answer: The reflected rays appear to diverge from a point behind the mirror, so the brain interprets the image as being behind the mirror.

questions.question: Describe how regular reflection differs from diffused reflection.
questions.answer: Regular reflection occurs on smooth surfaces producing clear images, while diffused reflection occurs on rough surfaces scattering light, producing no clear image.

questions.question: Explain why convex mirrors produce diminished images.
questions.answer: Convex mirrors diverge light rays, so the reflected rays appear to come from a smaller virtual image behind the mirror.

questions.question: Why do shadows form on the opposite side of the light source?
questions.answer: Shadows form where light is blocked, so the blocked region appears on the opposite side from the light source.

questions.question: Explain how lateral inversion occurs in a plane mirror.
questions.answer: Lateral inversion happens because left and right sides are reversed in the image, while top and bottom remain unchanged.

questions.question: Describe how a concave mirror is used in torches.
questions.answer: Concave mirrors focus light rays into a beam, making the light from the torch brighter and directed.

questions.question: Explain why the brightness of a shadow decreases as the light source is moved further away.
questions.answer: As the light source moves away, light spreads more, so less light is blocked completely, making the shadow fainter.

questions.question: Why does a rough surface scatter light in many directions?
questions.answer: The irregularities on the rough surface reflect light at different angles, causing scattering.

questions.question: Explain how a periscope allows viewing from a hidden position.
questions.answer: Light from an object is reflected by two mirrors into the observer’s eyes, allowing observation without a direct line of sight.

questions.question: Describe why images formed by a concave mirror can be real or virtual.
questions.answer: When the object is beyond the focal point, converging rays form a real image; within the focal point, rays diverge to form a virtual image.

questions.question: Explain why convex mirrors are preferred in traffic safety mirrors.
questions.answer: Convex mirrors provide a wider field of view, allowing drivers to see approaching vehicles from a distance.

questions.question: How does a sundial indicate time using shadows?
questions.answer: The shadow of the gnomon moves according to the Sun’s position, pointing to marked hours on the sundial.
